name: Route of Cebolla, Illán de Vacas
name_original: Cebolla, Illán de Vacas y Domingo Pérez – circular desde Montearagón
description: This 31.8-mile touring bicycle route from Montearagón explores the areas of Onion, Illán de Vacas, and Domingo Pérez, rated moderate.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Montearagón traverse a varied landscape, from semi-arid plains dotted with olive and cereal fields to the rugged backdrop of the Sierra de Gredos. The region is characterized by its karstic formations, dramatic canyons, and deep valleys where quiet rivers meander through forests. Cyclists can explore routes along the Alberche and Tagus riverbeds, as well as through pine forests, cliffs, and oak groves in the Pre-Pyrenean foothills. This diverse terrain offers a range of experiences for touring cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Montearagón The most popular touring cycling route is Route of Cebolla, Illán de Vacas, a 31.8 miles (51.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 3 minutes to complete. This route leads through semi-arid landscapes interspersed with olive and cereal fields. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Old Alberche Bridge – Talavera de la Reina loop from Montearagón, a difficult 66.8 miles (107.5 km) path. This path features historic landmarks like the Old Alberche Bridge and utilizes the Cordel de Merinas bike path. Local touring cyclists also love the Cazalegas Reservoir – Puerto del Piélago loop from Cazalegas, a 37.2 miles (59.8 km) trail leading through scenic views of the Cazalegas Reservoir with the Sierra de Gredos in the background, often completed in about 4 hours 16 minutes. Touring cycling around Montearagón is defined by semi-arid landscapes, karstic canyons, and Pre-Pyrenean valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to more challenging routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 90 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Montearagón's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Montearagón?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes around Montearagón, catering to various skill levels. This includes 2 easy routes, 6 moderate routes, and 12 difficult routes, ensuring options for every cyclist.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Montearagón?
A: The terrain around Montearagón is remarkably diverse. You'll find routes traversing semi-arid landscapes dotted with olive and cereal fields, as well as paths through rugged karstic formations, dramatic canyons, and deep valleys where quiet rivers like the Alberche and Tagus meander. The backdrop often includes the imposing Sierra de Gredos and the foothills of the Pre-Pyrenees, featuring pine forests, cliffs, and oak groves.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Montearagón?
A: Yes, Montearagón offers a few routes suitable for beginners. While many routes are moderate to difficult, there are at least 2 easy touring cycling routes available, perfect for those new to the sport or looking for a more relaxed ride.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see while touring cycling near Montearagón?
A: The region is rich in history. You can cycle past the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5061285" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Alberche Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, which still retains Roman pillars, or explore routes near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2746855" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cebolla Castle Ruins&lt;/a&gt;. The impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5451674" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Malpica Castle&lt;/a&gt;, surrounded by moats and the Tagus river, also makes for a scenic point of interest. Montearagón Castle itself, an abandoned castle-abbey from 1085, is a significant historical landmark in the area.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints along the touring cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. Many routes offer stunning vistas. For example, the route around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4158386" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazalegas Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; provides beautiful views with the Sierra de Gredos in the background. The parish church of San Cipriano, situated on a hill amidst olive and cereal fields, also offers picturesque views of the surrounding landscape.

Q: Can I explore natural parks or unique landscapes by touring bike in this region?
A: Yes, the region is close to the Sierra y Cañones de Guara Natural Park, renowned for its karstic landscapes, dramatic canyons, and gorges. While many routes within the park are geared towards mountain biking, the broader Aragonese Pre-Pyrenees region, where Montearagón is located, offers an epic quality for touring cyclists, with routes through rugged peaks, deep valleys, pine forests, cliffs, and oak groves.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Montearagón?
A: Yes, several circular routes are popular among touring cyclists. A notable one is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31894248" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Alberche Bridge – Talavera de la Reina loop from Montearagón&lt;/a&gt;, which is a challenging 66.8-mile (107.5 km) path. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20139689" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazalegas Reservoir – Puerto del Piélago loop from Cazalegas&lt;/a&gt;, a 37.2-mile (59.8 km) trail offering scenic views.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Montearagón?
A: The region's climate, with its semi-arid landscapes, generally makes spring and autumn ideal for touring cycling. Temperatures are milder, and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summer can be quite hot, especially in the plains, while winter might bring cooler temperatures, particularly in higher elevations.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring bike routes in Montearagón?
A: While many routes are moderate to difficult, families might enjoy sections of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5061314" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cordel de Merinas bike path&lt;/a&gt;. This ancient livestock route has been converted into a bike lane, offering a pleasant and generally flatter cycling experience suitable for various ages and skill levels, connecting towards Talavera de la Reina.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about touring cycling in Montearagón?
A: Touring cyclists frequently praise the diverse landscapes, from the tranquil riverbeds to the dramatic karstic formations. The blend of natural beauty with historical landmarks like the Montearagón Castle and the Old Alberche Bridge is also a significant draw. The variety of routes, catering to different fitness levels, ensures that both casual riders and experienced cyclists find something to enjoy.

Q: Are there longer, more challenging touring routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, Montearagón offers several challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31894248" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Alberche Bridge – Talavera de la Reina loop from Montearagón&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 66.8-mile (107.5 km) path. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31914850" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Water Spout – Senda Viriato (GR 63) loop from Cazalegas&lt;/a&gt;, covering over 60 km with significant elevation changes.

Q: Can I find routes that combine natural beauty with cultural exploration?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Montearagón seamlessly blend natural beauty with cultural exploration. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1022587130/route-of-cebolla-illan-de-vacas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of Cebolla, Illán de Vacas&lt;/a&gt; takes you through semi-arid landscapes while passing by the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2746855" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cebolla Castle Ruins&lt;/a&gt;. The region's ancient trails often connect forgotten medieval villages, offering a glimpse into local history and culture amidst stunning scenery.
tips: Farming village between olive groves and vineyards that celebrates a wine festival in summer. It is a classic rural enclave on the banks of the Tagus with low houses and a quiet atmosphere.

The parish church of San Cipriano stands on top of the hill where the town lies, standing out among olive and cereal fields irrigated with the waters of the nearby Tagus riverbed. Cebolla is part of the region of Talavera de la Reina and, among its narrow and twisting streets, you can find a variety of services for the intern.

With 3 registered people, Illán de Vacas is the municipality with the fewest inhabitants in Spain. There are other towns with the same or even less number of neighbors but they belong to other municipalities that together have more inhabitants.

Domingo Pérez's crops are those of the Mediterranean trilogy: cereals, vines and olive trees. Among the places of interest in the municipality are the Town Hall, an example of popular civil architecture; the hermitage of La Soledad which, located on a small hillock between two paths, was built in 1770; and the parish church of the Immaculate Conception, in a "rural Mudejar" style.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate. While it covers a significant distance of approximately 51 km, the elevation gain is around 369 meters, making it manageable for cyclists with a reasonable level of fitness. The terrain can vary, including both paved and unpaved sections, particularly around Montearagón.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain varies significantly across the different sections of this route. Near Montearagón, you can expect a mix of paved and unpaved surfaces, including gravel roads, characteristic of a semi-arid landscape. In the areas around Illán de Vacas and Domingo Pérez, the scenery typically features olive and cereal fields, with routes that might include both paved roads and agricultural tracks.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes by several notable landmarks. You can see the Monument to the Victims of COVID-19 and the Spanish Civil War in Montearagón, the historic Cebolla Castle Ruins, the Church of the Virgin of Peace of Illán de Vacas, and the Parish Church of the Immaculate Conception in Domingo Pérez.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. You'll find parts of the Camino Real de Guadalupe, the Valle del Tajo Cebolla, the Camino Natural del Tajo (including the GR 113 variant), the Camino de Illán de Vacas a Domingo Pérez, the Camino de las Cadenas, and the Sendero San Sebastián.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the semi-arid characteristics of some regions and the agricultural landscapes, spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) are generally the best times. Temperatures are milder, and the scenery is often at its most pleasant. Summers can be very hot, especially in central Spain, and winters can be chilly.

faqs.question: Is the route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: While the route is rated moderate, its length of over 50 km and varied terrain, including gravel sections, might be challenging for very young children or absolute beginners. Cyclists should have a good basic fitness level and be comfortable with longer distances and mixed surfaces. Shorter, dedicated family-friendly routes might be available closer to Montearagón or in the Toledo region.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed on public cycling routes in Spain, especially on rural and unpaved sections, provided they are kept under control, preferably on a leash, and that you clean up after them. However, specific rules might apply in certain protected areas or within village limits, so always be mindful of local signage and etiquette.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, this is a public touring bicycle route, and you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle it. However, if you plan to visit any specific attractions or historical sites along the way, they might have their own entrance fees.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route starting from Montearagón, you would typically look for parking in or near the village of Montearagón itself. Public parking options should be available within the village or at designated spots near the start of the route. Always check local signs for any restrictions.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food, drinks, or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through or near several villages, including Montearagón, Illán de Vacas, and Domingo Pérez. These villages are likely to have basic amenities such as small shops, cafes, or bars where you can refuel. For more substantial meals or accommodation, you would find more options in the larger towns nearby. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for longer stretches between villages.

faqs.question: What kind of bicycle is best suited for this route?
faqs.answer: Given the mixed terrain, including potential gravel and unpaved sections, a touring bicycle with wider tires or a gravel bike would be ideal. A mountain bike would also be suitable. A road bike might struggle on the unpaved segments, so it's best to choose a bike that can handle varied surfaces comfortably.
